questions.stem: A 76-year-old woman treated with IV levofloxacin for pneumonia has creatinine rise from 90 to 280 µmol/L on day 7. She is euvolemic. Urinalysis: 1+ protein, blood, WBCs and WBC casts; FENa 2.5%. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explain: AKI ~7–10 days after a culprit drug (fluoroquinolone) with WBCs and WBC casts is classic acute interstitial nephritis (allergic, tubulointerstitial). ATN would show muddy-brown granular casts; pre-renal would have low FENa and bland sediment.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old has 4 months of fatigue, weight loss, and arthralgia; BP 185/110; creatinine 510 (baseline 85), new normocytic anemia. Urine: heavy hematuria, 3 g/L protein, RBC casts. Which statement is most accurate?
options: Empiric IV (pulse) corticosteroids should be started urgently to preserve renal function
Withhold all treatment until biopsy confirms the exact etiology
Hemodialysis must start immediately to prevent nephron loss
A primary podocyte foot-process defect is the mechanism
questions.explain: Nephritic syndrome with rapidly rising creatinine and RBC casts = rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, a renal emergency. Start empiric IV pulse corticosteroids urgently — do not delay for biopsy.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman has fatigue, arthralgia, and new hemoptysis; BP 170/95. Urine shows heavy hematuria with RBC casts. ANA negative; C3 and C4 normal. Most likely mechanism?
options: Granulomatous inflammation with autoantibodies to neutrophil cytoplasm (pauci-immune)
IgA immune-complex deposition in the mesangium
questions.explain: A pulmonary-renal syndrome (hemoptysis + RBC casts) with negative ANA and normal complement is classic pauci-immune ANCA-associated vasculitis (anti-PR3 or anti-MPO). Low complement/positive ANA would suggest lupus.

questions.stem: A 71-year-old man is anuric (0 mL output) on post-op day 3 with creatinine 310 (baseline 95). His CKD-EPI calculator returns a number. What is his approximate true eGFR?
options: Cannot be estimated without a 24-hour urine
questions.explain: In anuric, non-steady-state AKI, the kidneys are filtering essentially nothing, so true eGFR ≈ 0 mL/min. Estimating equations are invalid because they assume a stable creatinine — they lag behind acute changes.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old with diabetic nephropathy (baseline Cr 210) has vomiting, can't eat, but keeps taking ramipril. K⁺ 7.6 mmol/L; ECG shows peaked T waves and widened QRS. Most appropriate first step?
questions.explain: With hyperkalemia and ECG changes (wide QRS), the first step is IV calcium gluconate to stabilize the myocardium (raises threshold potential). It does not lower K⁺ but buys time; insulin/dextrose, β2-agonists, and excretion/dialysis follow.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old mechanic is found confused in his garage with a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis, an osmolar gap of 38, and envelope-shaped crystals in the urine. Which toxic metabolite explains his renal findings?
questions.explain: High anion gap + high osmolar gap + envelope-shaped (calcium oxalate) crystals = ethylene glycol poisoning. It's metabolized to oxalic acid, which precipitates as calcium oxalate in the tubules → acute kidney injury. Formic acid is the methanol metabolite (causes blindness).

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman started on furosemide 3 weeks ago has weakness, orthostatic hypotension, undetectable JVP, and palpitations. Na 126, K 2.8, Cl 84, HCO₃ 34. Most appropriate primary step to correct her hyponatremia?
questions.explain: This is hypovolemic hyponatremia from diuretic-induced losses; low effective volume drives non-osmotic ADH release. Restoring volume with isotonic saline shuts off ADH and lets the kidney excrete free water. Hypertonic saline is reserved for acute severe neuro symptoms (e.g., seizures).

questions.stem: A 31-year-old with T1DM and 3 days of vomiting from gastroenteritis: pH 7.27, pCO₂ 26, Na 132, Cl 83, HCO₃ 10, glucose 19, urea 7, measured osmolality 290. Anion gap = 39; calculated osmolality = 290. What is the complete acid-base diagnosis?
options: Pure anion-gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate compensation
Non-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis
Anion-gap metabolic acidosis with a concurrent metabolic alkalosis
Anion-gap acidosis, metabolic alkalosis, and an osmolar gap
questions.explain: ΔAG = 39−12 = 27; expected HCO₃ would fall to ~24−27 = −3, yet measured HCO₃ is 10 — far higher than the acidosis alone predicts. That extra base reveals a concurrent metabolic alkalosis (from vomiting) on top of the DKA anion-gap acidosis. Osmolar gap is 0 (no toxic alcohol).

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with obstructive LUTS and a smooth, enlarged prostate is started on tamsulosin. His history includes a prior MI; he takes ramipril and atenolol. Most critical counseling point given his medications?
options: Reflex tachycardia worsening ischemic heart disease
Combining the alpha-blocker with his antihypertensives risks profound hypotension
High risk of acute urinary retention requiring catheterization
Permanent loss of libido from reduced DHT
questions.explain: Alpha-1 blockers (tamsulosin) cause vasodilation and orthostatic hypotension. Stacked on an ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker, the additive blood-pressure drop can cause syncope — counsel on first-dose/orthostatic hypotension and dose separation. (DHT reduction/libido loss relates to 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors.)

questions.stem: A 34-year-old man has a firm, painless, slowly enlarging intratesticular nodule; history of surgically corrected cryptorchidism. Most appropriate next step?
options: Scrotal ultrasound plus serum AFP and β-hCG
questions.explain: A solid, painless intratesticular mass (cryptorchidism is a risk factor) is presumed malignant until proven otherwise. Work up with scrotal ultrasound + tumour markers (AFP, β-hCG). Incisional/needle biopsy is contraindicated — it risks seeding tumour into scrotal lymphatics.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman who is 24 weeks pregnant is diagnosed with gestational hypertension (155/95 on repeated readings). Which antihypertensive is strictly contraindicated?
questions.explain: ACE inhibitors (ramipril) and ARBs are teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. Safe options include labetalol, methyldopa, and long-acting nifedipine.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy has 1 week of periorbital and leg edema with normal blood pressure. Dipstick shows 4+ protein and is negative for blood. Most appropriate next step?
options: Start oral prednisone (1 mg/kg) with close outpatient follow-up
ACE inhibitor and loop diuretic, reassess in 4 weeks
questions.explain: Pediatric nephrotic syndrome (heavy proteinuria, edema, no hematuria) is overwhelmingly minimal change disease, which is highly steroid-responsive. Start empiric prednisone; biopsy is reserved for steroid non-responders.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with ESRD on hemodialysis has missed sessions and now reports fatigue, pruritus, and bone pain. Which lab pattern fits the expected mineral-bone disorder (CKD-MBD)?
options: High calcium, low phosphate, low PTH
Low calcium, low phosphate, high PTH
Low calcium, high phosphate, high PTH
High calcium, high phosphate, low PTH
questions.explain: In CKD-MBD, loss of 1-alpha-hydroxylase lowers active vitamin D (→ low calcium), the kidney can't excrete phosphate (→ high phosphate), and both stimulate the parathyroids (→ high PTH, secondary hyperparathyroidism).

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with Stage 4 CKD has worsening fatigue and dyspnea; Hb 88 g/L (was 105), iron stores adequate. You start an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent. Most appropriate target hemoglobin?
questions.explain: ESA therapy is indicated for symptomatic anemia with Hb < 100 g/L; the target is 110–120 g/L. Normalizing Hb (>130) is avoided because it raises stroke risk.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with pulmonary edema has creatinine 550 (baseline 130), K⁺ 7.4, and venous pH 7.05. After IV insulin/glucose and high-dose loop diuretics he remains anuric with K⁺ 7.2. Most definitive next step?
questions.explain: Refractory hyperkalemia + severe acidosis (pH < 7.1) + anuric volume overload meets multiple AEIOU dialysis criteria. Failure of medical therapy mandates urgent hemodialysis for definitive removal.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man with advanced dementia who depends on staff for hydration becomes progressively unresponsive over 48 hours with dry mucous membranes and a serum sodium of 158 mmol/L. Primary derangement?
options: Absolute excess of total body sodium
Relative deficit of total body water
Failure of the collecting duct to excrete potassium
questions.explain: Hypernatremia is a relative water deficit. Thirst normally protects against it, so it develops in those who can't access water (dementia, infants, the bedbound). The fix is free water (oral/NG or IV D5W).

questions.stem: A 71-year-old woman on furosemide for 3 days develops palpitations; ECG shows flattened T waves and prominent U waves. By what mechanism is the diuretic causing this?
options: Blocking the Na-Cl cotransporter in the distal convoluted tubule
Blocking the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb
Antagonizing aldosterone in the collecting duct
Inhibiting carbonic anhydrase in the proximal tubule
questions.explain: Furosemide is a loop diuretic that blocks the Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in the thick ascending limb, driving distal flow and urinary potassium wasting → hypokalemia (flat T waves, U waves). Option A = thiazides; C = MRAs; D = acetazolamide.

questions.stem: A 15-year-old boy has sudden severe left scrotal pain and swelling that woke him from sleep. Exam shows a high-riding testis with a horizontal lie, a thickened cord, and an absent cremasteric reflex. Best management?
options: Scrotal ultrasound with tumor markers, then follow-up
questions.explain: Sudden pain with a horizontal lie and absent cremasteric reflex is testicular torsion — a surgical emergency. Proceed to urgent surgical exploration/detorsion to salvage the testis; do not delay for imaging when the diagnosis is clinically clear.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old man has worsening obstructive LUTS plus new deep, aching, non-positional lower-back pain that wakes him at night. DRE reveals a hard, irregular, asymmetric nodule. A TRUS-guided biopsy is done. Which finding confirms the likely diagnosis?
options: Malignant glandular proliferation graded by the Gleason system
questions.explain: Hard irregular nodule + axial bone pain suggests metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma (peripheral zone), histologically malignant glandular proliferation graded by the Gleason score (sum of the two most common patterns). Benign hyperplasia would indicate BPH.

cards.prompt: AKI 8 days after starting a fluoroquinolone; urine shows WBCs and WBC casts, with eosinophilia. Diagnosis?
cards.explain: Drug exposure 7–10 days earlier + WBC casts + eosinophilia = acute interstitial nephritis. Stop the drug ± steroids.

cards.prompt: Match each urinary cast to its lesion:
cards.explain: Casts are nearly free diagnoses — anchor your AKI/glomerular reasoning on them.

cards.text: Prerenal AKI: FENa is below {{1}}%, the BUN:creatinine ratio is above {{20}}, the sediment is {{bland}}, and it reverses once perfusion is restored.
cards.explain: FENa is unreliable on diuretics — use FEurea (< 35%) instead.

cards.prompt: Nephritic syndrome (RBC casts) with hemoptysis; ANA negative and complement normal. Most likely mechanism?
cards.explain: Pulmonary-renal syndrome with normal complement and negative ANA = pauci-immune ANCA vasculitis (or anti-GBM). Low complement/positive ANA would suggest lupus.

cards.prompt: Which nephritic causes have LOW complement? (select all)
cards.explain: Low complement: post-strep, lupus, MPGN, cryoglobulinemia. Normal complement: IgA, ANCA, anti-GBM.

cards.prompt: Order the treatment of hyperkalemia with ECG changes:
cards.explain: Calcium first — it stabilizes the myocardium but does not lower K⁺; shifting and excretion follow.

cards.text: CKD mineral-bone disorder shows {{low}} calcium, {{high}} phosphate, and {{high}} PTH, with low active (1,25) vitamin D.
cards.explain: Loss of 1-α-hydroxylase + phosphate retention + ↑FGF23 drive secondary hyperparathyroidism.

cards.prompt: Symptomatic anemia of CKD, iron replete, starting an ESA. Target hemoglobin?
cards.explain: Target 110–120 g/L; normalizing Hb above 130 increases stroke/CV risk.

cards.prompt: Why does a volume-depleted patient become hyponatremic even with normal kidneys — and what fluid corrects it?
cards.answer: Low effective circulating volume triggers non-osmotic ADH release, so the kidney retains free water despite a low sodium. Restoring volume with isotonic saline switches off ADH and lets the kidney excrete the excess water. (Hypertonic saline is reserved for acute severe neuro symptoms.)
cards.explain: Hypovolemic hyponatremia: UNa helps localize renal vs extrarenal loss; treat with isotonic saline.

cards.text: Hypernatremia is a {{water}} deficit; it happens in those who can't access water. Correct it {{slowly}} to avoid cerebral {{edema}}.
cards.explain: Mirror image: chronic hyponatremia corrected too fast → osmotic demyelination.

cards.prompt: High anion-gap acidosis, high osmolar gap, and envelope-shaped crystals in the urine. Toxin?
cards.explain: Ethylene glycol → oxalic acid → calcium-oxalate crystals + AKI. Methanol → formic acid → blindness. Treat with fomepizole + dialysis.

items: Identify the primary disorder (metabolic vs respiratory)
Delta-delta for a hidden second disorder
Osmolar gap if high anion gap
cards.explain: Always compute the gap even if HCO₃ is normal — the delta-delta unmasks a coexisting metabolic alkalosis (DKA + vomiting).

cards.prompt: Match each diuretic to site / signature effect:
cards.explain: "Loops Lose calcium; thiazides reTain calcium." K-sparing agents raise potassium.

cards.prompt: Adolescent with sudden severe scrotal pain, a high-riding horizontal testis, and an absent cremasteric reflex. Best step?
cards.explain: Classic testicular torsion — go straight to surgical exploration/detorsion within ~6 h; don't delay for imaging.
Torsion → surgery; infected obstruction → emergency decompression (stent/nephrostomy); Fournier → debridement. Simple cystitis is outpatient.

guides.title: Topic 12 AKI
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 12 ◇

Acute Kidney Injury

KU 1 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Definition & the pre/intra/post framework
AKI (KDIGO): ↑creatinine ≥26.5 µmol/L in 48 h, or ≥1.5× baseline within 7 d, or urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for ≥6 h.
Three buckets: prerenal (hypoperfusion, reversible), intrinsic (ATN/AIN/glomerular/vascular), postrenal (obstruction). Always get a renal ultrasound + bladder scan to exclude obstruction, and review the med list.
Urine tells the story: bland = prerenal/postrenal; muddy-brown granular casts = ATN; WBC casts = AIN; RBC casts = glomerular.
FENa: <1% prerenal, >1–2% ATN — but unreliable on diuretics (use FEurea <35%), and low in contrast/glomerular/early obstruction.
★ AKI emergencies
Hyperkalemia with ECG changes — IV calcium gluconate first (membrane stabilization) → insulin/dextrose + salbutamol (shift) → saline/furosemide or binder (excrete) → dialysis if refractory.
Dialyze for AEIOU — Acidosis (refractory pH<7.1), Electrolytes (refractory hyperkalemia), Intoxication (methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates), Overload (refractory pulmonary edema), Uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, bleeding).

Prerenal

Causes true volume loss (hemorrhage, GI, diuretics); ↓effective volume (CHF, cirrhosis, sepsis); hemodynamic — NSAIDs (afferent constriction), ACEi/ARB (efferent dilation), hepatorenal
Labs FENa <1%, UNa <20, urine osm >500, BUN:Cr >20, bland sediment
Key feature reverses with restoration of perfusion; prolonged → ATN

Intrinsic — ATN & AIN

ATN — ischemic prolonged prerenal/shock
ATN — nephrotoxic contrast, aminoglycosides, amphotericin, cisplatin; myoglobin (rhabdo), hemoglobin, urate (tumor lysis), myeloma casts
ATN clues muddy-brown granular casts, FENa >1–2%; phases oliguric → polyuric recovery
AIN drugs (NSAIDs, PPIs, β-lactams, sulfa, allopurinol) ~7–10 d later; fever/rash/eosinophilia (triad often absent); WBC casts, sterile pyuria → stop drug ± steroids
Vascular TTP/HUS, scleroderma renal crisis, cholesterol emboli (post-cath, livedo, blue toes, eosinophilia), malignant HTN

Postrenal (obstruction)

Causes BPH, bilateral stones, pelvic malignancy, retroperitoneal fibrosis, neurogenic bladder (needs bilateral or solitary-kidney obstruction)
Diagnose renal US → hydronephrosis; bladder scan
Relieve outlet (BPH) → urethral catheter; ureteric → stent/nephrostomy
Watch post-obstructive diuresis — monitor fluids/electrolytes

Work-up & complications

Work-up urinalysis + microscopy (casts), FENa/FEurea, renal US, bladder scan, med review, volume assessment, CK if rhabdo suspected
Rhabdomyolysis ↑CK, dipstick blood+ but no RBCs (myoglobin), ↑K/PO₄, ↓Ca → aggressive fluids
Complications
Watch for hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, volume overload, hyperphosphatemia, uremia (pericarditis, encephalopathy, platelet dysfunction)

Management & pitfalls

General treat the cause, optimize perfusion/volume, stop nephrotoxins, renally dose drugs, manage K⁺/acidosis
Contrast nephropathy prevent with hydration; distinguish from cholesterol emboli (delayed, eosinophilia)
Pitfalls
FENa on diuretics use FEurea instead
Anuric AKI eGFR ≈ 0 — equations invalid in non-steady state
Triple whammy NSAID + ACEi/ARB + diuretic → prerenal AKI
Always exclude obstruction US/bladder scan early
Topic 12 / Acute Kidney Injury (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 13 Glomerular Disease
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 13 ◇

Glomerular Disease

KU 1 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
★ The one distinction that organizes everything: nephrotic vs nephritic
NephroticNephritic
Lesionpodocyte injury (non-proliferative)inflammation/proliferation
Proteinuria>3.5 g/day (heavy)mild–moderate (<3.5)
Sedimentbland; oval fat bodies/fatty castshematuria + RBC casts, dysmorphic RBCs
Otherhypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemiaHTN, oliguria, ↓GFR
★ Glomerular emergency — RPGN
Rapidly progressive GN = nephritic picture (RBC casts) with creatinine climbing over days–weeks; crescents on biopsy. Three immune patterns: anti-GBM (linear IgG), immune-complex (granular — lupus, post-strep, IgA), pauci-immune (ANCA). Start empiric IV pulse corticosteroids ± cyclophosphamide urgently — do NOT wait for biopsy; add plasmapheresis for anti-GBM.

Nephrotic syndrome

Definition proteinuria >3.5 g/day + hypoalbuminemia + edema + hyperlipidemia/lipiduria (Maltese crosses)
Complications hypercoagulable (loss of antithrombin III → renal vein thrombosis/PE), infection (Ig loss), hyperlipidemia
Manage ACEi/ARB (↓proteinuria), diuretics, statin, treat cause; anticoagulate if indicated

Nephrotic causes

Minimal change children; foot-process effacement on EM; steroid-responsive; idiopathic / NSAIDs / Hodgkin
FSGS commonest in adults (esp. Black ancestry); HIV, obesity, reflux; segmental sclerosis
Membranous anti-PLA2R; "spike and dome" subepithelial deposits; secondary (malignancy, hep B, SLE, drugs); very hypercoagulable
Diabetic Kimmelstiel-Wilson nodules; commonest overall
Amyloid Congo red, apple-green birefringence

Nephritic syndrome

Picture hematuria (dysmorphic RBCs, RBC casts), HTN, oliguria, ↓GFR, sub-nephrotic proteinuria, edema
Key reflex check complement to split the differential

Nephritic causes — by complement

LOW complement post-streptococcal (↓C3, 1–3 wk post-GAS, subepithelial humps, resolves), MPGN, lupus nephritis (↓C3/C4, anti-dsDNA), cryoglobulinemia (hep C), endocarditis
NORMAL complement IgA nephropathy (commonest GN; synpharyngitic hematuria 1–2 d after URI), IgA vasculitis/HSP, ANCA-associated (pauci-immune), anti-GBM/Goodpasture (hemoptysis)
IgA vs post-strep (classic discriminator)
IgA hematuria with/1–2 d after URI, normal complement
Post-strep hematuria 1–3 wk after, ↓C3, ↑ASO

The vasculitides (pulmonary-renal)

Anti-GBM / Goodpasture linear IgG, hemoptysis + RBC casts, anti-GBM Ab → plasmapheresis + steroids + cyclophosphamide
GPA c-ANCA/PR3; sinus + lung + kidney
MPA p-ANCA/MPO; lung + kidney
EGPA asthma + eosinophilia + p-ANCA
All ANCA pauci-immune, normal complement

Work-up & pitfalls

Work-up urinalysis/microscopy, protein quantification (UACR/PCR), serologies (ANA, dsDNA, complement, ANCA, anti-GBM, ASO, hep B/C, cryoglobulins), biopsy
Pitfalls
Casts RBC = nephritic; fatty/oval-fat = nephrotic
Membranous screen for malignancy/secondary causes; high VTE risk
RPGN renal emergency — treat empirically before biopsy
Normal complement + hemoptysis think ANCA or anti-GBM (not lupus)
Topic 13 / Glomerular Disease (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 14 CKD and Complications
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 14 ◇

Chronic Kidney Disease & Complications

KU 1 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
Definition, staging & causes
CKD: eGFR <60 or markers of kidney damage (albuminuria, hematuria, structural) for ≥3 months.
GFR stages: G1 ≥90 (+damage), G2 60–89, G3a 45–59, G3b 30–44, G4 15–29, G5 <15 (ESRD). Albuminuria: A1 <3, A2 3–30, A3 >30 mg/mmol.
Causes: diabetes #1, hypertension #2, glomerulonephritis, ADPKD (flank pain, hematuria, HTN, hepatic cysts, berry aneurysms), obstructive.
CV disease is the #1 cause of death in CKD — CKD is a cardiovascular risk multiplier.

Slowing progression

Blood pressure target ~<130/80
ACEi / ARB first-line with albuminuria (↓intraglomerular pressure, ↓proteinuria); accept ≤30% Cr rise, monitor K⁺
SGLT2 inhibitor slows progression in diabetic AND non-diabetic CKD (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin)
Also glycemic control, sodium/protein moderation, avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, contrast)

CKD-MBD (mineral-bone)

Mechanism ↓1-α-hydroxylase → ↓1,25-vit D; phosphate retention; ↑FGF23 → ↓Ca → secondary hyperPTH
Lab pattern ↓Ca, ↑phosphate, ↑PTH, ↓1,25-vit D
Treat dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binders (non-calcium e.g. sevelamer) + active vit D (calcitriol) + cinacalcet (lowers PTH & Ca) → prevents renal osteodystrophy

Anemia of CKD

Mechanism EPO deficiency → normocytic normochromic anemia
First ensure iron repletion before starting an ESA
ESA target Hb ~100–115 g/L (don't normalize >130 — ↑stroke/CV risk)

Other complications

Metabolic acidosis ↓ammonia excretion → treat with oral sodium bicarbonate (slows progression, protects bone/muscle)
Hyperkalemia ↓excretion; diet, binders; balance against ACEi/ARB benefit
Volume / HTN salt restriction, diuretics
Uremia (late) pericarditis, encephalopathy, platelet dysfunction, pruritus, restless legs
CV disease leading cause of death — aggressive risk-factor control

Kidney replacement

When (AEIOU) refractory Acidosis, Electrolytes (hyperK), Intoxication, Overload, Uremia — or progressive ESRD with symptoms
Access create AV fistula early (preferred over catheter); "fistula first"
Modalities hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis
Transplant best survival/quality of life when eligible

Pitfalls

eGFR equations assume steady state — invalid in AKI/non-steady state
ACEi/ARB expect ≤30% Cr bump; stop if larger or hyperK; contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis and pregnancy
Don't over-correct anemia Hb >130 increases harm
CKD = CV risk equivalent treat lipids/BP aggressively
ADPKD screen for berry aneurysm if family history of subarachnoid hemorrhage
Topic 14 / CKD & Complications (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 15 Fluids and Electrolytes
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 15 ◇

Fluids & Electrolytes

KU 2 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
The concept that unlocks sodium problems
Sodium content = ECF volume (edema vs dehydration). Sodium concentration = water balance / cell size. They're independent — a patient can be volume-overloaded and hyponatremic at once.
Dysnatremias are water problems: hyponatremia = relative water excess; hypernatremia = water deficit. ADH (driven by osmolality and by low effective volume) is the central lever.
Correct slowly: chronic hyponatremia too fast → osmotic demyelination; hypernatremia too fast → cerebral edema. Target ≤8–10 mmol/L/24 h.
Refractory K⁺ or Ca²⁺? Check and replace magnesium.
★ Electrolyte emergencies
Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure, coma) — 3% hypertonic saline bolus to raise Na a few mmol/L acutely; otherwise correct slowly to avoid osmotic demyelination.
Severe hyperkalemia + ECG changes — IV calcium gluconate first (stabilize) → insulin/dextrose + salbutamol (shift) → furosemide/binder/dialysis (excrete). ECG: peaked T → wide QRS → sine wave.

Hyponatremia — the algorithm

① Osmolality normal/high → pseudohyponatremia (lipids/paraproteins) or hyperglycemia (translocational; correct Na +~2.4/5.5 mmol glucose), mannitol
② If hypotonic → volume
Hypovolemic renal (diuretics, salt-wasting, adrenal — UNa>20) vs extrarenal (GI, third-space — UNa<20) → isotonic saline
Euvolemic SIADH, hypothyroid, glucocorticoid deficiency, psychogenic polydipsia/beer potomania (dilute urine) → fluid restriction
Hypervolemic CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic, renal failure (↓effective volume → ADH) → salt + water restriction, treat cause

SIADH

Criteria euvolemic hypotonic hyponatremia, urine osm >100 (inappropriately concentrated), UNa >30, low uric acid, normal thyroid/adrenal
Causes CNS (stroke, tumor), pulmonary (pneumonia, SCLC), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine), pain/post-op, malignancy
Treat fluid restriction; salt ± loop; vaptans/demeclocycline for refractory

Hypernatremia

Mechanism water deficit — thirst normally protects, so occurs in those without water access (elderly, infants, AMS) or with DI
Causes pure water loss (insensible, central/nephrogenic DI), hypotonic loss (GI, osmotic diuresis), rarely Na gain
Treat free water (oral/NG or IV D5W); correct slowly; address DI (desmopressin if central)

Potassium

Hyperkalemia
Causes ↓excretion (renal failure, ACEi/ARB, K-sparing, type 4 RTA/hypoaldo, Addison), shift (acidosis, insulin deficiency, cell lysis/rhabdo/tumor lysis, β-blocker, digoxin), pseudo (hemolyzed sample)
ECG peaked T → ↑PR/↓P → wide QRS → sine → VF
Hypokalemia
Causes GI loss, renal (diuretics, hyperaldo, Bartter/Gitelman, type 1/2 RTA, Mg deficiency), shift (alkalosis, insulin, β-agonist, refeeding)
ECG flat T, U waves, ST depression
Treat KCl; correct Mg (refractory hypoK)

Mg · PO₄ · pitfalls

Hypomagnesemia diuretics, PPI, alcohol, diarrhea → refractory hypoK/hypoCa, torsades → replace Mg
Hypermagnesemia renal failure/antacids → ↓reflexes, resp depression → Ca gluconate
Hypophosphatemia refeeding, DKA treatment, alcohol → weakness, rhabdo, hemolysis
Pitfalls
Correct slowly chronic hypoNa (ODS); hyperNa (cerebral edema)
Hyperglycemia → pseudohyponatremia; correct the Na
Vomiting → hypokalemia mainly via renal loss (bicarbonaturia + aldosterone), not the vomitus
RTA & K type 4 → hyperK; types 1/2 → hypoK
Topic 15 / Fluids & Electrolytes (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 16 Acid Base
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 16 ◇

Acid–Base

KU 2 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
★ The stepwise approach — do it every time, in order
pH: acidemia (<7.35) or alkalemia (>7.45)?
Primary process: metabolic (HCO₃ moves with pH) or respiratory (pCO₂ moves opposite pH)?
Compensation appropriate? Metabolic acidosis → Winter's: expected pCO₂ = 1.5×HCO₃ + 8 (±2). Over/under-shoot = a second disorder.
Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO₃) (normal ~12). Always compute it, even if HCO₃ is normal.
If high gap → delta-delta (ΔAG / ΔHCO₃): ~1–2 pure HAGMA; <1 added NAGMA; >2 added metabolic alkalosis.
If high gap → osmolar gap for toxic alcohols (measured − calculated osm >10).

High anion-gap metabolic acidosis

GOLDMARK / MUDPILES Glycols (ethylene/propylene), Oxoproline, L-/D-Lactate, Methanol, Aspirin, Renal/uremia, Ketoacidosis
Lactic acidosis sepsis/shock, ischemia, metformin
Ketoacidosis DKA, alcoholic, starvation
Osmolar gap >10 → toxic alcohol
Ethylene glycol → oxalic acid → Ca-oxalate crystals, AKI
Methanol → formic acid → blindness
Treat fomepizole + dialysis

Normal anion-gap (hyperchloremic)

Causes GI bicarbonate loss (diarrhea), renal tubular acidosis, carbonic anhydrase inhibitors, saline infusion
Urine anion gap (UNa+UK−UCl): negative = GI loss (kidney appropriately excretes NH₄⁺); positive = renal (RTA)
Renal tubular acidosis
Type 1 (distal) can't excrete H⁺; hypokalemia, urine pH >5.5, stones
Type 2 (proximal) HCO₃ wasting; hypokalemia; Fanconi
Type 4 hypoaldosteronism → hyperkalemia, ↓NH₄⁺

Metabolic alkalosis

Chloride-responsive (urine Cl <20) vomiting/NG suction (HCl loss), diuretics, contraction → corrects with saline
Chloride-resistant (urine Cl >20) hyperaldosteronism, Cushing, Bartter/Gitelman, severe hypokalemia
Maintained by volume depletion, hypokalemia, hyperaldosteronism

Respiratory disorders

Acidosis (↑pCO₂) hypoventilation — COPD, sedation/opioids, neuromuscular, obesity-hypoventilation
Compensation acute HCO₃ ↑1 per 10 ↑pCO₂; chronic ↑~3.5–4 per 10
Alkalosis (↓pCO₂) hyperventilation — anxiety/pain, hypoxia, PE, sepsis, salicylates, pregnancy, high altitude

Mixed disorders (high-yield)

DKA + vomiting HAGMA + metabolic alkalosis (delta-delta >2)
Salicylate toxicity HAGMA + respiratory alkalosis (the classic mixed picture)
COPD + diuretic/vomiting respiratory acidosis + metabolic alkalosis
Sepsis lactic (HAGMA) + early respiratory alkalosis

Pitfalls

Always check compensation over/under-shoot reveals a second disorder
Compute the gap even if HCO₃ normal can hide a mixed HAGMA + alkalosis
Delta-delta unmasks a concurrent metabolic alkalosis (DKA + vomiting)
Salicylates think mixed acid-base, not a single disorder
Osmolar gap for any unexplained HAGMA → toxic alcohol
Topic 16 / Acid–Base (detailed) · generated from your 11-week guides · verify against course & clinical guidelines · review aid only

guides.title: Topic 17 Diuretics
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 17 ◇

Diuretics

KU 2 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
★ Where each class acts along the nephron
ClassSiteTargetSignature electrolyte effect
Carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (acetazolamide)PCTcarbonic anhydraseNAGMA (HCO₃ loss), hypokalemia
Osmotic (mannitol)PCT/looposmotic↓ICP; pulmonary edema risk
Loop (furosemide)thick ascending limbNa-K-2Cl↓K, ↓Mg, ↓Ca, metabolic alkalosis
Thiazide (HCTZ, chlorthalidone)distal convoluted tubuleNa-Cl↓K, ↓Na, ↑Ca, ↑urate/glucose/lipids
K-sparing — ENaC (amiloride)collecting ductENaC↑K, mild acidosis
K-sparing — MRA (spironolactone)collecting ductaldosterone receptor↑K, gynecomastia

Loop diuretics

Agents furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide; ethacrynic acid if sulfa allergy
Mechanism block Na-K-2Cl in TAL — most potent; also ↑Ca excretion
Uses CHF/edema, pulmonary edema, HTN with renal impairment, hyperkalemia, hypercalcemia (with saline)
SE hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypocalcemia, contraction metabolic alkalosis, ototoxicity (rapid IV/with aminoglycosides), hyperuricemia, hypovolemia

Thiazides

Agents HCTZ, chlorthalidone, indapamide, metolazone
Mechanism block Na-Cl in DCT; retain calcium
Uses HTN (first-line), CHF, nephrolithiasis (↓urine Ca), nephrogenic DI
SE ("hyper-GLUC") ↑Glucose, ↑Lipids, ↑Uric acid (gout), ↑Calcium; plus hypoNa (esp. elderly), hypoK, hypomagnesemia; ↑lithium levels
Note less effective at eGFR <30 → use a loop

Potassium-sparing & MRAs

ENaC blockers (amiloride, triamterene) collecting-duct Na channel; uses: with loop/thiazide to spare K, Liddle, lithium-induced nephrogenic DI
MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone, finerenone) aldosterone-receptor block; uses: HFrEF (mortality), resistant HTN, primary aldosteronism, cirrhotic ascites
SE hyperkalemia (esp. with ACEi/ARB), metabolic acidosis; spironolactone → gynecomastia (eplerenone spares this)

Others

Acetazolamide glaucoma, altitude sickness, urine alkalinization; causes NAGMA
Mannitol ↑ICP, glaucoma; avoid in pulmonary edema/anuria
SGLT2 inhibitors mild osmotic diuresis + glycosuria; CV/renal protection
Vaptans (tolvaptan) V2-receptor antagonist — SIADH, ADPKD

Clinical use pearls

Diuretic resistance sequential nephron blockade — add a thiazide (metolazone) to a loop
Cirrhotic ascites spironolactone + furosemide (~100:40 ratio)
HFrEF loop for symptoms + MRA for mortality
Stone prevention thiazide reduces calcium stones (lowers urine Ca)

Pitfalls

Sulfa allergy loops & thiazides are sulfonamides → ethacrynic acid alternative
Loop vs thiazide & calcium Loops Lose Ca; thiazides reTain Ca
K direction loops/thiazides ↓K; K-sparing/MRA ↑K
MRA + ACEi/ARB monitor for hyperkalemia
Spironolactone gynecomastia + teratogenic — counsel

guides.title: Topic 18 Urology
guides.html:
◇ Topic Reference · 18 ◇

Urology

KU 2–3 · ~2 pp
trigger → answer
★ Urologic emergencies
Testicular torsion — sudden severe pain, high-riding/horizontal testis, absent cremasteric reflex, negative Prehn → urgent surgical exploration within ~6 h. Don't delay for imaging.
Obstructing stone + infection (fever, obstruction on imaging) → urosepsis → emergency decompression (ureteric stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) + antibiotics.
Fournier gangrene — necrotizing fasciitis of the perineum (diabetics) → resuscitation, broad antibiotics, urgent surgical debridement.

BPH & LUTS

Pathology transition-zone enlargement → bladder outlet obstruction
Symptoms voiding (hesitancy, weak stream, intermittency, incomplete emptying) + storage (frequency, urgency, nocturia); IPSS
Complications retention, UTI, stones, hydronephrosis/post-renal AKI
α-blocker (tamsulosin) rapid relief; SE orthostatic hypotension (additive with antihypertensives), retrograde ejaculation, floppy-iris
5α-reductase inhibitor (finasteride) shrinks large glands over ~6 mo; ↓libido/ED, halves PSA, teratogenic
Refractory TURP; acute retention → catheter (see AKI: decompress at the outlet)

Prostate cancer

Pathology peripheral-zone adenocarcinoma; hard nodule on DRE, ↑PSA
Spread osteoblastic bone metastases → back pain
Grade Gleason score / grade groups
Screening PSA — shared decision-making (controversial)
Localized active surveillance (low risk), radical prostatectomy, or radiation
Metastatic androgen deprivation (GnRH agonist/antagonist ± antiandrogen); cover agonist flare with antiandrogen

Testicular: the three-way split

Torsion sudden, severe, horizontal lie, absent cremasteric, negative Prehn → surgery (emergency)
Epididymitis gradual, fever, dysuria, positive Prehn (relief on elevation); young = STI (GC/chlamydia), older = coliform → antibiotics
Tumor painless hard mass, young men, cryptorchidism risk → US + AFP/β-hCG/LDH → radical inguinal orchiectomy; NEVER trans-scrotal biopsy

Nephrolithiasis

Stone types calcium oxalate (most common); uric acid (radiolucent, acidic urine); struvite (infection/Proteus → staghorn); cystine
Presentation acute renal colic (flank → groin), hematuria, nausea
Diagnose non-contrast CT (gold standard); US
Manage hydration, analgesia (NSAIDs), medical expulsive therapy (α-blocker) for distal stones <10 mm; most <5 mm pass
Admit / urgent urology
If obstruction + infection (decompress!), solitary kidney, AKI, intractable pain/vomiting, large stone
Prevent hydration; thiazide (calcium stones), citrate, allopurinol (uric acid)

UTI & prostatitis

Cystitis dysuria, frequency, urgency → nitrofurantoin / TMP-SMX / fosfomycin
Pyelonephritis fever, flank pain, CVA tenderness → fluoroquinolone or IV; image if obstruction suspected
Complicated men, pregnancy, catheter, obstruction, immunocompromise
Acute bacterial prostatitis fever, dysuria, tender boggy prostate — don't massage; E. coli → fluoroquinolone/TMP-SMX
Asymptomatic bacteriuria treat ONLY in pregnancy or before urologic procedures

Hematuria, bladder Ca & incontinence

Painless gross hematuria (older/smoker) urothelial bladder cancer until proven → CT urogram + cystoscopy + cytology
Glomerular vs urologic dysmorphic RBCs/casts/protein = glomerular; isomorphic = urologic
Stress leak with cough/exertion → pelvic floor exercises, surgery
Urge / OAB detrusor overactivity → antimuscarinic (oxybutynin — contraindicated in narrow-angle glaucoma), mirabegron, behavioral
Overflow retention/obstruction or neurogenic → catheter, treat cause

Pitfalls

Torsion operate — don't wait for ultrasound
Testicular mass US + markers; never trans-scrotal biopsy (seeding)
Stone + infection emergency decompression, not just antibiotics
GnRH agonist flare cover with antiandrogen in prostate cancer
α-blocker orthostatic hypotension stacked on antihypertensives
PDE5 inhibitors contraindicated with nitrates
